Search results: 



--- SOURCE 1: Clinical promise and applications of epigenetic biomarkers ---
URL: https://clinicalepigeneticsjournal.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8

SUMMARY:
We define the current progress in the development of epigenetic biomarkers for clinical use in cancer as well as in other common complex diseases and aging.

FULL CONTENT:
Advertisement

# Clinical promise and applications of epigenetic biomarkers

[*Clinical Epigenetics*](/)
**volume 16**, Article number: 192 (2024)
[Cite this article](#citeas)

2655 Accesses

1 Citations

2 Altmetric

[Metrics details](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8/metrics)

Epigenetic dysregulation is involved in a wide spectrum of diseases, and many studies have reported differential molecular epigenetic signatures between diseased tissues/cells and healthy control samples [[1](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR1 "Berdasco M, Esteller M. Clinical epigenetics: seizing opportunities for translation. Nat Rev Genet. 2019;20(2):109–27."), [2](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR2 "Hanahan D. Hallmarks of cancer: new dimensions. Cancer Discov. 2022;12(1):31–46.")]. Such epigenetic signals may point to promising therapeutic targets, but may also have great potential as biomarkers. Indeed, increasing evidence shows that epigenetic biomarkers have potential for prediction [[3](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR3 "Butler M, Pongor L, Su YT, Xi L, Raffeld M, Quezado M, et al. MGMT status as a clinical biomarker in glioblastoma. Trends Cancer. 2020;6(5):380–91.")], early diagnosis [[4](#ref-CR4 "de Waard J, Bhattacharya A, de Boer MT, van Hemel BM, Esajas MD, Vermeulen KM, et al. Identification of a methylation panel as an alternative triage to detect CIN3+ in hrHPV-positive self-samples from the population-based cervical cancer screening programme. Clin Epigenetics. 2023;15(1):103."),[5](#ref-CR5 "Lu H, Liu Y, Wang J, Fu S, Wang L, Huang C, et al. Detection of ovarian cancer using plasma cell-free DNA methylomes. Clin Epigenetics. 2022;14(1):74."),[6](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR6 "Herzog C, Sundström K, Jones A, Evans I, Barrett JE, Wang J, et al. DNA methylation-based detection and prediction of cervical intraepithelial neoplasia grade 3 and invasive cervical cancer with the WID™-qCIN test. Clin Epigenetics. 2022;14(1):150.")] or prognosis [[7](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR7 "Šestáková Š, Cerovská E, Šálek C, Kundrát D, Ježíšková I, Folta A, et al. A validation study of potential prognostic DNA methylation biomarkers in patients with acute myeloid leukemia using a custom DNA methylation sequencing panel. Clin Epigenetics. 2022;14(1):22.")] of disease, archiving a life course of environmental exposures [[8](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR8 "Chamberlain JD, Nusslé S, Chapatte L, Kinnaer C, Petrovic D, Pradervand S, et al. Blood DNA methylation signatures of lifestyle exposures: Tobacco and alcohol consumption. Clin Epigenetics. 2022;14(1):155."), [9](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR9 "Kim K, Zheng Y, Joyce BT, Jiang H, Greenland P, Jacobs DR Jr, et al. Relative contributions of six lifestyle- and health-related exposures to epigenetic aging: the Coronary Artery Risk Development in Young Adults (CARDIA) Study. Clin Epigenetics. 2022;14(1):85.")], as well as stratifying for and monitoring of therapy [[3](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR3 "Butler M, Pongor L, Su YT, Xi L, Raffeld M, Quezado M, et al. MGMT status as a clinical biomarker in glioblastoma. Trends Cancer. 2020;6(5):380–91.")]; some have linked epigenetic variability to predictions of mortality [[10](#ref-CR10 "Lund JB, Li S, Baumbach J, Svane AM, Hjelmborg J, Christiansen L, et al. DNA methylome profiling of all-cause mortality in comparison with age-associated methylation patterns. Clin Epigenetics. 2019;11(1):23."),[11](#ref-CR11 "Huan T, Nguyen S, Colicino E, Ochoa-Rosales C, Hill WD, Brody JA, et al. Integrative analysis of clinical and epigenetic biomarkers of mortality. Aging Cell. 2022;21(6): e13608."),[12](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR12 "Morrow JD, Make B, Regan E, Han M, Hersh CP, Tal-Singer R, et al. DNA methylation is predictive of mortality in current and former smokers. Am J Respir Crit Care Med. 2020;201(9):1099–109.")]. Although in the field of oncology, various commercially available kits already exist [[1](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR1 "Berdasco M, Esteller M. Clinical epigenetics: seizing opportunities for translation. Nat Rev Genet. 2019;20(2):109–27."), [13](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR13 "Clarke SJ, Molley PL. Early insights into cancer epigenetics: gene promoter hypermethylation emerges as a potential biomarker for cancer detection. Cancer Res. 2022;82(8):1461–3.")], clinical applications are lagging behind the discoveries of new biomarkers in other common diseases. In this thematic series of *Clinical Epigenetics*, we define the current progress in the development of epigenetic biomarkers for clinical use in cancer as well as in other common complex diseases and aging and include a variety of contributions ranging from original research to opinion pieces [[14](#ref-CR14 "Dupras C, Knoppers T, Palmour N, Beauchamp E, Liosi S, Siebert R, et al. Researcher perspectives on ethics considerations in epigenetics: an international survey. Clin Epigenetics. 2022;14(1):110."),[15](#ref-CR15 "Santaló J, Berdasco M. Ethical implications of epigenetics in the era of personalized medicine. Clin Epigenetics. 2022;14(1):44."),[16](#ref-CR16 "Chiapperino L, Paneni F. Why epigenetics is (not) a biosocial science and why that matters. Clin Epigenetics. 2022;14(1):144."),[17](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR17 "Qi L, Teschendorff AE. Cell-type heterogeneity: Why we should adjust for it in epigenome and biomarker studies. Clin Epigenetics. 2022;14(1):31.")] and reviews [[18](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR18 "Massen M, Lommen K, Wouters KAD, Vandersmissen J, van Criekinge W, Herman JG, et al. Technical considerations in PCR-based assay design for diagnostic DNA methylation cancer biomarkers. Clin Epigenetics. 2022;14(1):56."), [19](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR19 "Cristoferi I, Giacon TA, Boer K, van Baardwijk M, Neri F, Campisi M, et al. The applications of DNA methylation as a biomarker in kidney transplantation: a systematic review. Clin Epigenetics. 2022;14(1):20.")]. In assembling this collection, we also highlight critical issues related to the Ethical, Legal and Social Implications (ELSI) of epigenetics research [[14](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR14 "Dupras C, Knoppers T, Palmour N, Beauchamp E, Liosi S, Siebert R, et al. Researcher perspectives on ethics considerations in epigenetics: an international survey. Clin Epigenetics. 2022;14(1):110."), [15](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR15 "Santaló J, Berdasco M. Ethical implications of epigenetics in the era of personalized medicine. Clin Epigenetics. 2022;14(1):44.")]. This includes the growing responsibility for us as scientists to investigate and uncover the mechanisms relating social determinants to human health [[16](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR16 "Chiapperino L, Paneni F. Why epigenetics is (not) a biosocial science and why that matters. Clin Epigenetics. 2022;14(1):144.")] as technology advances and social inequities persist. Although epigenetic research and progress has accelerated dramatically in the past decade, the interest in applications of epigenetic age analysis in the realm of life insurance, direct to consumer testing and confirmation of juvenile-age for asylum (immigration) [[14](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR14 "Dupras C, Knoppers T, Palmour N, Beauchamp E, Liosi S, Siebert R, et al. Researcher perspectives on ethics considerations in epigenetics: an international survey. Clin Epigenetics. 2022;14(1):110.")], highlights the obligation of engaged scientists to promote research standards and provide balanced interpretations of epigenetics observations.

In this *Clinical Epigenetics* series on epigenetic biomarkers, the range of topics (Fig. [1](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#Fig1)) supports the growing clinical translational relevance of epigenetic research, especially research on DNA methylation variability. Although best developed for cancer, the emergence of high-throughput assays has supported the development of epigenetic epidemiological research of (fetal) exposures and other common diseases.

![figure 1](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1186%2Fs13148-024-01806-8/MediaObjects/13148_2024_1806_Fig1_HTML.png)

Word cloud showing the range of topics in the Clinical Epigenetics series on Epigenetic Biomarkers

The current collection of articles addresses fetal origins of complex diseases [[20](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR20 "Yang MN, Huang R, Zheng T, Dong Y, Wang WJ, Xu YJ, et al. Genome-wide placental DNA methylations in fetal overgrowth and associations with leptin, adiponectin and fetal growth factors. Clin Epigenetics. 2022;14(1):192.")], lifestyle associations with the epigenome [[8](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR8 "Chamberlain JD, Nusslé S, Chapatte L, Kinnaer C, Petrovic D, Pradervand S, et al. Blood DNA methylation signatures of lifestyle exposures: Tobacco and alcohol consumption. Clin Epigenetics. 2022;14(1):155."), [9](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR9 "Kim K, Zheng Y, Joyce BT, Jiang H, Greenland P, Jacobs DR Jr, et al. Relative contributions of six lifestyle- and health-related exposures to epigenetic aging: the Coronary Artery Risk Development in Young Adults (CARDIA) Study. Clin Epigenetics. 2022;14(1):85."), [21](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR21 "Si J, Chen L, Yu C, Guo Y, Sun D, Pang Y, et al. Healthy lifestyle, DNA methylation age acceleration, and incident risk of coronary heart disease. Clin Epigenetics. 2023;15(1):52."), [22](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR22 "Nwanaji-Enwerem JC, Chung FF, Van der Laan L, Novoloaca A, Cuenin C, Johansson H, et al. An epigenetic aging analysis of randomized metformin and weight loss interventions in overweight postmenopausal breast cancer survivors. Clin Epigenetics. 2021;13(1):224.")], as well as predictive marks of heart disease [[9](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR9 "Kim K, Zheng Y, Joyce BT, Jiang H, Greenland P, Jacobs DR Jr, et al. Relative contributions of six lifestyle- and health-related exposures to epigenetic aging: the Coronary Artery Risk Development in Young Adults (CARDIA) Study. Clin Epigenetics. 2022;14(1):85."), [21](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR21 "Si J, Chen L, Yu C, Guo Y, Sun D, Pang Y, et al. Healthy lifestyle, DNA methylation age acceleration, and incident risk of coronary heart disease. Clin Epigenetics. 2023;15(1):52."), [23](#ref-CR23 "Kim JY, Jelinek J, Lee YH, Kim DH, Kang K, Ryu SH, et al. Altered methylation pattern in EXOC4 is associated with stroke outcome: an Epigenome- Wide Association Study. Aorta-specific DNA methylation patterns in cell-free DNA from patients with bicuspid aortic valve-associated aortopathy. Clin Epigenetics. 2021;13(1):147.")] and cancer [[5](#ref-CR5 "Lu H, Liu Y, Wang J, Fu S, Wang L, Huang C, et al. Detection of ovarian cancer using plasma cell-free DNA methylomes. Clin Epigenetics. 2022;14(1):74."),[6](#ref-CR6 "Herzog C, Sundström K, Jones A, Evans I, Barrett JE, Wang J, et al. Clin Epigenetics. 2022;14(1):150."),[7](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR7 "Šestáková Š, Cerovská E, Šálek C, Kundrát D, Ježíšková I, Folta A, et al. Clin Epigenetics. 2022;14(1):22."), [22](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR22 "Nwanaji-Enwerem JC, Chung FF, Van der Laan L, Novoloaca A, Cuenin C, Johansson H, et al. An epigenetic aging analysis of randomized metformin and weight loss interventions in overweight postmenopausal breast cancer survivors. Clin Epigenetics. 2021;13(1):224."), [28](#ref-CR28 "Beynon RA, Ingle SM, Langdon R, May M, Ness A, Martin RM, et al. Epigenetic biomarkers of ageing are predictive of mortality risk in a longitudinal clinical cohort of individuals diagnosed with oropharyngeal cancer. Clin Epigenetics. 2021;13(1):232."),[30](#ref-CR30 "Zhou J, Cheng T, Li X, Hu J, Li E, Ding M, et al. Epigenetic imprinting alterations as effective diagnostic biomarkers for early-stage lung cancer and small pulmonary nodules. Novel blood-based FUT7 DNA methylation is associated with lung cancer: especially for lung squamous cell carcinoma. Clin Epigenetics. 2022;14(1):167.")]. Epigenetic marks of cancers have advanced beyond complex disease, and this special collection highlights several innovations including the application of cell-free DNA methylomes providing insights to ovarian cancer [[5](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR5 "Lu H, Liu Y, Wang J, Fu S, Wang L, Huang C, et al. Detection of ovarian cancer using plasma cell-free DNA methylomes. Clin Epigenetics. 2022;14(1):74.")], a custom sequencing panel for acute myeloid leukemia [[7](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR7 "Šestáková Š, Cerovská E, Šálek C, Kundrát D, Ježíšková I, Folta A, et al. A validation study of potential prognostic DNA methylation biomarkers in patients with acute myeloid leukemia using a custom DNA methylation sequencing panel. Clin Epigenetics. 2022;14(1):22.")], as well as advances with promise of clinical translation for lung [[32](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR32 "Fang Y, Qu Y, Ji L, Sun H, Li J, Zhao Y, et al. Novel blood-based FUT7 DNA methylation is associated with lung cancer: especially for lung squamous cell carcinoma. Clin Epigenetics. 2022;14(1):167.")], breast [[22](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR22 "Nwanaji-Enwerem JC, Chung FF, Van der Laan L, Novoloaca A, Cuenin C, Johansson H, et al. An epigenetic aging analysis of randomized metformin and weight loss interventions in overweight postmenopausal breast cancer survivors. Clin Epigenetics. 2021;13(1):224.")], renal [[31](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR31 "Joosten SC, Odeh SNO, Koch A, Buekers N, Aarts MJB, Baldewijns MMLL, et al. Development of a prognostic risk model for clear cell renal cell carcinoma by systematic evaluation of DNA methylation markers. Clin Epigenetics. 2021;13(1):103.")], colorectal [[29](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR29 "Lin WH, Xiao J, Ye ZY, Wei DL, Zhai XH, Xu RH, et al. Circulating tumor DNA methylation marker MYO1-G for diagnosis and monitoring of colorectal cancer. Clin Epigenetics. 2021;13(1):232.")] or oropharyngeal cancers [[28](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR28 "Beynon RA, Ingle SM, Langdon R, May M, Ness A, Martin RM, et al. Epigenetic biomarkers of ageing are predictive of mortality risk in a longitudinal clinical cohort of individuals diagnosed with oropharyngeal cancer. Clin Epigenetics. 2022;14(1):1.")]. This compendium on cancer also includes a three gene test for cervical intraepithelial neoplasia and cervical cancer [[6](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR6 "Herzog C, Sundström K, Jones A, Evans I, Barrett JE, Wang J, et al. DNA methylation-based detection and prediction of cervical intraepithelial neoplasia grade 3 and invasive cervical cancer with the WID™-qCIN test. Clin Epigenetics. 2022;14(1):150.")]. As the consideration of a clinical translational role for epigenetic biomarkers matures, we have also observed the potential relevance of fetal programming of adult diseases through the evaluation of placental methylomes [[20](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR20 "Yang MN, Huang R, Zheng T, Dong Y, Wang WJ, Xu YJ, et al. Genome-wide placental DNA methylations in fetal overgrowth and associations with leptin, adiponectin and fetal growth factors. Clin Epigenetics. 2022;14(1):192.")], and advancements of insights to capture impacts of both social determinants [[16](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR16 "Chiapperino L, Paneni F. Why epigenetics is (not) a biosocial science and why that matters. Clin Epigenetics. 2022;14(1):144.")] and lifestyle exposures [[8](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR8 "Chamberlain JD, Nusslé S, Chapatte L, Kinnaer C, Petrovic D, Pradervand S, et al. Blood DNA methylation signatures of lifestyle exposures: Tobacco and alcohol consumption. Clin Epigenetics. 2022;14(1):155."), [9](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR9 "Kim K, Zheng Y, Joyce BT, Jiang H, Greenland P, Jacobs DR Jr, et al. Relative contributions of six lifestyle- and health-related exposures to epigenetic aging: the Coronary Artery Risk Development in Young Adults (CARDIA) Study. Clin Epigenetics. 2022;14(1):85.")] on the epigenome. For clinical applications to nonneoplastic disease, multiple studies [[21](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR21 "Si J, Chen L, Yu C, Guo Y, Sun D, Pang Y, et al. Healthy lifestyle, DNA methylation age acceleration, and incident risk of coronary heart disease. Clin Epigenetics. 2023;15(1):52."), [23](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR23 "Kim JY, Jelinek J, Lee YH, Kim DH, Kang K, Ryu SH, et al. Hypomethylation in MTNR1B: a novel epigenetic marker for atherosclerosis profiling using stenosis-radiophenotype and blood inflammatory cells. Clin Epigenetics. 2023;15(1):11."), [24](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR24 "Zhang X, Wang C, He D, Cheng Y, Yu L, Qi D, et al. Identification of DNA methylation-regulated genes as potential biomarkers for coronary heart disease via machine learning in the Framingham Heart Study. Clin Epigenetics. 2022;14(1):122.")] have addressed the relevance of peripheral methylomes to cardiovascular disease, including the calculation of epigenetic clocks [[21](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR21 "Si J, Chen L, Yu C, Guo Y, Sun D, Pang Y, et al. Healthy lifestyle, DNA methylation age acceleration, and incident risk of coronary heart disease. Clin Epigenetics. 2023;15(1):52.")]. Methylation biomarkers in neurologic diseases and syndromes using leukocyte DNA holds particular promise as evidenced through the prediction of stroke risk [[26](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR26 "Liu C, Li M, Yin Q, Fan Y, Shen C, Yang R. HTRA1 methylation in peripheral blood as a potential marker for the preclinical detection of stroke: a case-control study and a prospective nested case-control study. Clin Epigenetics. 2022;14(1):191.")], characterization of stroke outcomes [[25](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR25 "Cullell N, Soriano-Tárraga C, Gallego-Fábrega C, Cárcel-Márquez J, Muiño E, Llucià-Carol L, et al. Altered methylation pattern in EXOC4 is associated with stroke outcome: an Epigenome- Wide Association Study. Clin Epigenetics. 2022;14(1):124.")], as well as the prediction of multiple sclerosis severity through the development of epigenetic risk scores [[33](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR33 "Campagna MP, Xavier A, Lea RA, Stankovich J, Maltby VE, Butzkueven H, et al. Whole blood methylation signatures are associated with and accurately classify multiple sclerosis disease severity. Clin Epigenetics. 2022;14(1):194.")]. Applications to ulcerative colitis [[34](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR34 "Venkateswaran S, Somineni HK, Matthews JD, Kilaru V, Hyams JS, Denson LA, et al. Longitudinal DNA methylation profiling of the rectal mucosa identifies cell-specific signatures of disease status, severity and clinical outcomes in ulcerative colitis cell-specific DNA methylation signatures of UC. Clin Epigenetics. 2023;15(1):50.")] highlight opportunities for pharmaco-epigenetic targeting in nonneoplastic disease, here immune cell targets, and considerations for organ transplantation [[19](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR19 "Cristoferi I, Giacon TA, Boer K, van Baardwijk M, Neri F, Campisi M, et al. The applications of DNA methylation as a biomarker in kidney transplantation: a systematic review. Clin Epigenetics. 2022;14(1):20.")] may transform the clinical landscape related to posttransplant complications.

## DNA methylation biomarkers

Although histone modifications and DNA hydroxymethylation might prove useful epi-markers in the future, so far DNA methylation has been exploited most for epigenetic biomarker development mainly due to relatively high stability of this epigenetic modification. A variety of methods are available to measure differences in DNA methylation. Most assays make use of bisulfite conversion before the methylation analysis, although emerging technologies avoid this potentially harsh DNA treatment in favor of bisulfite-free approaches [[35](#ref-CR35 "Boers R, Boers J, de Hoon B, Kockx C, Ozgur Z, Molijn A, et al. Genome-wide DNA methylation profiling using the methylation-dependent restriction enzyme LpnPI. Genome Res. 2018;28(1):88–99."),[36](#ref-CR36 "Liu Y, Wang X, Li Y, Wu H. An all-in-one strategy for bisulfite-free DNA methylation detection by temperature-programmed enzymatic reactions. Anal Chim Acta. 2023;1251: 341001."),[37](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR37 "Bendixen KK, Mindegaard M, Epistolio S, Dazio G, Marchi F, Spina P, et al. A qPCR technology for direct quantification of methylation in untreated DNA. Nat Commun. 2023;14(1):5153.")]. For single gene analysis, the most common assays are (quantitative) methylation-specific PCR ((Q)MSP), bisulfite pyrosequencing, combined bisulfite restriction analysis (COBRA), targeted bisulfite sequencing and methylation-sensitive high-resolution melting (MS-HRM) [[38](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR38 "Laird PW. Principles and challenges of genomewide DNA methylation analysis. Nat Rev Genet. 2010;11(3):191–203."), [39](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR39 "Bock C, Halbritter F, Carmona FJ, Tierling S, Datlinger P, Assenov Y, et al. Quantitative comparison of DNA methylation assays for biomarker development and clinical applications. Nat Biotechnol. 2016;34(7):726–37.")]. For diagnostic methylation assays of specific genes, QMSP is the most commonly used technique followed by bisulfite pyrosequencing [[38](#ref-CR38 "Laird PW. Principles and challenges of genomewide DNA methylation analysis. Nat Rev Genet. 2010;11(3):191–203."),[39](#ref-CR39 "Bock C, Halbritter F, Carmona FJ, Tierling S, Datlinger P, Assenov Y, et al. Quantitative comparison of DNA methylation assays for biomarker development and clinical applications. Nat Biotechnol. 2016;34(7):726–37."),[40](#ref-CR40 "Taryma-Leśniak O, Sokolowska KE, Wojdacz TK. Current status of development of methylation biomarkers for in vitro diagnostic IVD applications. Clin Epigenetics. 2020;12(1):100."),[41](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR41 "Eads CA, Danenberg KD, Kawakami K, Saltz LB, Blake C, Shibata D, et al. MethyLight: a high-throughput assay to measure DNA methylation. Nucleic Acids Res. 2000;28(8):E32.")] and can be regarded as established techniques applicable for biomarker development and clinical implementation [[39](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR39 "Bock C, Halbritter F, Carmona FJ, Tierling S, Datlinger P, Assenov Y, et al. Quantitative comparison of DNA methylation assays for biomarker development and clinical applications. Nat Biotechnol. 2016;34(7):726–37.")].QMSP is a specific and sensitive method that allows accurate quantification, high-throughput testing and only requires a minimal amount of input DNA [[40](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR40 "Taryma-Leśniak O, Sokolowska KE, Wojdacz TK. Current status of development of methylation biomarkers for in vitro diagnostic IVD applications. Clin Epigenetics. 2020;12(1):100."), [41](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR41 "Eads CA, Danenberg KD, Kawakami K, Saltz LB, Blake C, Shibata D, et al. MethyLight: a high-throughput assay to measure DNA methylation. Nucleic Acids Res. 2000;28(8):E32.")]. The advantage of bisulfite pyrosequencing is that it provides an absolute level of methylation by determining the ratio of methylated and unmethylated cytosine residues separately [[39](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR39 "Bock C, Halbritter F, Carmona FJ, Tierling S, Datlinger P, Assenov Y, et al. Quantitative comparison of DNA methylation assays for biomarker development and clinical applications. Nat Biotechnol. 2016;34(7):726–37."), [42](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR42 "Mikeska T, Felsberg J, Hewitt CA, Dobrovic A. Analysing DNA methylation using bisulphite pyrosequencing. Methods Mol Biol. 2011;791:33–53.")]. Disadvantages of the bisulfite pyrosequencing are that it is less sensitive to quantify methylated CpGs from dysplastic cells in an environment of many normal cells as found in multiple body materials, is only possible when sufficient CpG-free flanking sequences are available for primer design and is relatively expensive [[42](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR42 "Mikeska T, Felsberg J, Hewitt CA, Dobrovic A. Analysing DNA methylation using bisulphite pyrosequencing. Methods Mol Biol. 2011;791:33–53.")]. The analytical sensitivity of the methylation detection assay provided by PCR-based methods appears to be critical for diagnostic applications of epimutation testing. For example, using the same samples, MS-HRM was able to detect *BRCA1* epimutations, whereas the Illumina BeadChip microarray was not [[43](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR43 "Machaj F, Sokolowska KE, Borowski K, Retfiński S, Strapagiel D, Sobalska-Kwapis M, et al. Analytical sensitivity of a method is critical in detection of low-level BRCA1 constitution epimutation. Sci Rep. 2023;13(1):16102.")].

Analysis on the genome-wide level can be achieved by methylation (micro)arrays preceded by bisulfite conversion, such as EPIC arrays, immunoprecipitation of methylated DNA combined with next-generation sequencing and genome-wide bisulfite sequencing. Since the introduction of standard arrays allowing genome-wide interrogation of methylation over 10 years ago, epigenome-wide association studies (EWAS) have become a popular approach to identify biomarkers for both environmental exposures and disease outcomes [[44](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR44 "Wei S, Tao J, Xu J, Chen X, Wang Z, Zhang N, et al. Ten years of EWAS. Adv Sci (Weinh). 2021;8(20): e2100727."), [45](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR45 "Campagna MP, Xavier A, Lechner-Scott J, Maltby V, Scott RJ, Butzkueven H, et al. Epigenome-wide association studies: current knowledge, strategies and recommendations. Clin Epigenetics. 2021;13(1):214.")]. Case–control or prospective (longitudinal) cohort studies are the most widely used designs for the discovery of epigenetic biomarkers in EWAS [[45](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR45 "Campagna MP, Xavier A, Lechner-Scott J, Maltby V, Scott RJ, Butzkueven H, et al. Epigenome-wide association studies: current knowledge, strategies and recommendations. Clin Epigenetics. 2021;13(1):214.")]. To ensure the quality of reporting of such studies, general reporting guidelines on observational studies in epidemiology as specified in the STROBE (STrengthening the REporting of OBservational Studies in Epidemiology) statement should be used (see also: <https://www.equator-network.org/reporting-guidelines/>). However, reporting guidelines for observational studies such as case–control or cohort designs as specified in the STROBE checklist [[46](/articles/10.1186/s13148-024-01806-8#ref-CR46 "von Elm E, Altman DG, Egger M, Pocock SJ, Gøtzsche PC, Vandenbroucke JP. The Strengthening the Reporting of Observational Studies in Epidemiology (STROBE) statement: guidelines for reporting observational studies. Clin Epidemiol. 2008;61(4):344–9.")] are very general and guidelines specific for EWAS do not exist.

Similar to genome-wide association studies (GWAS), the early years of EWAS were characterized by a lack of consensus on data preparation and quality control, statistical analysis and reporting standards, including issues related to replication, multiple co

Review

Epigenetic biomarkers for disease susceptibility and preventative medicine
==================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Michael K.Skinner 1

Show more

Outline

Add to Mendeley

Open archive

Summary
-------

The development of molecular biomarkers for disease makes it possible for preventative medicine approaches to be considered. Therefore, therapeutics, treatments, or clinical management can be used to delay or prevent disease development. The problem with [genetic mutations](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/gene-mutation "Learn more about genetic mutations from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") as biomarkers is the low frequency with genome-wide association studies (GWASs), generally at best a 1% association of the patients with the disease. In contrast, epigenetic alterations have a high-frequency association of greater than 90%–95% of individuals with pathology in epigenome-wide association studies (EWASs). A wide variety of human diseases have been shown to have epigenetic biomarkers that are disease specific and that detect pathology susceptibility. This review is focused on the epigenetic biomarkers for disease susceptibility, and it distinct from the large literature on [epigenetics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/epigenomics "Learn more about epigenetics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of disease etiology or progression. The development of efficient epigenetic biomarkers for disease susceptibility will facilitate a paradigm shift from reactionary medicine to preventative medicine.

Introduction
------------

The first molecular mechanism identified as the regulatory process to control biology was [genetics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/genetics "Learn more about genetics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). The [DNA sequence](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/dna-sequence "Learn more about DNA sequence from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and the mutation of the sequence have been thought to be the cause for all aspects of biology, from evolutionary biology to disease development. This concept is termed “genetic determinism” and has been the focus of nearly all biological and medical research concepts for over a 100 years.[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1550413123004473#bib1) The sequencing of the [human genome](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/human-genome "Learn more about human genome from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in 2000 was heralded as the most significant advance in the biological and medical sciences and was thought to provide the solution for most biological and medical questions and problems.[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1550413123004473#bib2) This led to genome-wide association studies (GWASs) to identify the key DNA sequence mutations in biology and disease. Nearly every disease investigated was found to have associated [genetic mutations](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/gene-mutation "Learn more about genetic mutations from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") from these GWASs. The problem or issue that developed was the extremely low frequency of genetic mutations associated with the majority of disease conditions. The frequency of genetic mutations associated with the vast majority of disease was generally less than 1% of the diseased population.[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1550413123004473#bib3) So, at best, one out of 100 people with the disease would have an associated DNA sequence mutation. Although a small number of rare disease conditions (e.g., cystic fibrosis) do have higher frequency DNA sequence mutation associations, none of the high-frequency conditions, such as cancer, neurodegenerative disease, or metabolic disease, have been shown to have a high frequency of genetic mutation associations. For example, a world-wide analysis of obesity with thousands of patients was only found to have an associated DNA sequence mutation in approximately 2% of the population with obesity analyzed.[4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1550413123004473#bib4) Similar observations have also been made in other areas of biology and medicine as well. For evolutionary biology, the analysis of related species genetic mutations finds low-frequency correlations with the evolutionary process,[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1550413123004473#bib5) while epigenetic change has much higher frequency associations. It is not that genetics is not important; genetics is essential for all biological systems from bacteria to humans. It is simply that specific genetic mutations do not appear to be the driver for most of the biological or medical issues investigated. Without the DNA sequence, life would not exist, but the genetic deterministic view or premise alone is simply not accurate. Additional molecular processes distinct from genetics and DNA sequence are needed to address these failures of [genetic determinism](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/genetic-determinism "Learn more about genetic determinism from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages").

In the 1950s, the term “epigenetics” was coined to describe environmental-exposure-induced physical traits and inheritance observations that could not be explained with genetics.[6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1550413123004473#bib6),[7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1550413123004473#bib7),[8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1550413123004473#bib8) In the 1970s, the first epigenetic process of DNA [methylation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/methylation "Learn more about methylation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") was identified.[6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1550413123004473#bib6) In the 1980s and 1990s, further advances described [DNA methylation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/dna-methylation "Learn more about DNA methylation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [histone modification](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/histone-modification "Learn more about histone modification from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and [chromatin structure](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/chromatin-structure "Learn more about chromatin structure from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") as epigenetic processes. In the 2000s, [noncoding RNA](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/non-coding-rna "Learn more about noncoding RNA from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (ncRNA) was also developed as an epigenetic process.[6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1550413123004473#bib6) Likely many more distinct epigenetic processes will be identified in the future. The current definition of [epigenetics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/epigenomics "Learn more about epigenetics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is “molecular factors and processes around DNA that regulate genome activity, independent of DNA sequence, and are mitotically stable The primary function of epigenetics is to regulate gene expression. An example is DNA methylation when hypermethylated can shut off gene expression and hypomethylation can allow genes to turn on expression. In addition, specific types of chromatin histone modifications such as methylation turn off gene expression or others such as [acetylation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/acetylation "Learn more about acetylation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") turn on gene expression.[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1550413123004473#bib9) This corresponds to forms of chromatin structure such as [heterochromatin](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/heterochromatin "Learn more about heterochromatin from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (i.e., tightly packaged) that turns gene expression off or [euchromatin](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/euchromatin "Learn more about euchromatin from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (i.e., open chromatin) which allows gene expression to occur.[10](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1550413123004473#bib10) The ncRNA helps recruit the transcription factors or [chromatin remodeling](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/chromatin-remodeling "Learn more about chromatin remodeling from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") proteins to the DNA and facilitate these epigenetic processes.[11](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1550413123004473#bib11) Although ncRNA are expressed from DNA sequence and so require genetics, their actions are epigenetic by definition.[12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1550413123004473#bib12) Examples of the integration of these processes involves [ncRNA directed DNA methylation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/rna-directed-dna-methylation "Learn more about ncRNA directed DNA methylation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") or DNA-methylation-directed chromatin structures.[13](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1550413123004473#bib13)

Epigenetics is now known to be essential in the regulation of cellular and [physiological processes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/physiological-process "Learn more about physiological processes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Essentially, epigenetics regulates the genetic process of transcription and allows genes to be on or off to influence the levels of gene expression. The vast majority of examples of cell or developmental biology are regulated by the integrated actions of epigenetics and genetics. A good example is cellular specificity and function. There are over 270 different cell types in the human body, and all cell types have essentially the same genetic DNA sequence. Although infrequent genetic alterations, such as repeat elements, inversions, copy-number variation, and translocations, can vary between cell types, the vast majority of the genome is the same between cell types. So, how do we have 270 different cell types? Each cell type has a unique epigenetics that determines or drives the cell specificity.[14](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1550413123004473#bib14) So, each of the 270 cell types has a unique epigenetics that determines the unique gene-expression profile to allow the cell type to have a specific function and developmental profile.[15](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1550413123004473#bib15) Similarly, developmental biology processes have been shown to have unique transcriptional regulation and factors to promote the development of specific cell types or tissues. The epigenetic process is what allows the expression of these specific transcription factors to occur and response genes to be expressed. So, epigenetics is also required to allow and drive the development of cell and tissue formation. The development of a cell type or group of cell types in a tissue requires a cascade of developmental events to occur on the epigenetic level to progress from a stem cell state to a differentiated cell state. Therefore, all cells, tissues, organs, and organisms (e.g., from microbes to humans) require this integration of epigenetics and genetics to allow the normal physiology and biology to develop and exist.

In the event of an alteration in the normal epigenetic programming for a cell or organ system, there can be a corresponding change in a gene-expression event that can cause the susceptibility of pathology or disease to develop later in life. This process helps explain early-life impacts on later-life disease through the process of the “developmental origins of health and disease” (DOHAD).[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1550413123004473#bib16) Early in life an alteration in the epigenetics can cause a cascade of epigenetic and associated gene expression events throughout development to create the disease susceptibility and later-life pathology development. Although initially it was thought that this could be due to genetic mutations, the low frequency and lack of GWAS associations now suggests that this is not a critical factor in the development of most diseases. In contrast, the frequency of epigenetic alterations can be orders of magnitude in higher frequency than that of genetic mutations.[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1550413123004473#bib17) In examination of epigenetic alterations (i.e., epimutations) with abnormal pathology or disease shows the majority (i.e., >90%) of the individuals have correlated [epimutations](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/epimutation "Learn more about epimutations from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Therefore, in contrast to genetics, epigenetics and epimutations can exist in the vast majority of pathology or disease conditions. This is not only relevant to disease susceptibility and development but to other processes such as evolutionary biology, where high-frequency epimutations may promote [phenotypic variation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/phenotypic-variation "Learn more about phenotypic variation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and physiology traits that can become adaptive and facilitate evolution.[18](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1550413123004473#bib18) Therefore, epigenetics acts at high frequency within a population to regulate normal biology. When epimutations develop they influence disease susceptibility and pathology.

Environmental epigenetics
-------------------------

The development of Darwinian evolutionary biology theory in the late 1800s was based on the role of the environment in [natural selection](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/natural-selection "Learn more about natural selection from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), but not have phenotype directly influenced by the environment.[19](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1550413123004473#bib19) In the early 1900s, this was further developed with the chemical composition and eventually structure of the DNA. Therefore, the environment over the past 100 years has not been considered to play an active role in directly altering the molecular regulation of biology of the organism, except through natural selection. This [genetic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/genetics "Learn more about genetic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") deterministic paradigm that has developed only considers the environment as an external factor that can transiently influence the physiology or biology, but not influence or program the molecular control mechanisms.

In contrast, [epigenetics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/epigenomics "Learn more about epigenetics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") was discovered due to the ability of the environment to directly alter the epigenetic regulation and programming of the [epigenome](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/epigenome "Learn more about epigenome from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). The early examples are Paul Kammerer in 1910 who observed that the aquatic environment influences egg laying and reproduction in the midwife toad,[20](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1550413123004473#bib20) or heat exposure of the fruit fly causing wing shape change by Conrad Waddington in the 1950s.[6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1550413123004473#bib6),[7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1550413123004473#bib7) These examples led to the initial proposal that epigenetics exists. Therefore, epigenetics evolved along with [DNA sequence](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/dna-sequence "Learn more about DNA sequence from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") to provide a mechanism to facilitate the environmental regulation of biology. The majority of epigenetic studies have generally used various environmental factors to regulate or alter [epigenetic mechanisms](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/epigenetic-mechanism "Learn more about epigenetic mechanisms from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") such as [DNA methylation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/dna-methylation "Learn more about DNA methylation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [histone modifications](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/histone-modification "Learn more about histone modifications from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), or [chromatin structure](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/chromatin-structure "Learn more about chromatin structure from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). All epigenetic mechanisms and processes are environmentally responsive.[6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1550413123004473#bib6) Therefore, the environment through epigenetic regulation alters the [genetic mechanism](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/genetic-mechanism "Learn more about genetic mechanism from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of gene expression to impact the cell and [biological process](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/biological-phenomena-and-functions-concerning-the-entire-organism "Learn more about biological process from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Both epigenetics and genetics are essential and neither process can exist or function alone.

All environmental factors, from temperature, diet, toxicants, stress, and therapeutics, can regulate epigenetic processes to influence the biology and susceptibility to pathology development. Caloric restriction or high-fat diets in mammals and humans have been shown to alter the metabolic status and disease (e.g., obesity) of the individual through epigenetic change.[21](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1550413123004473#bib21),[22](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1550413123004473#bib22) Cold or heat exposure can impact a variety of [physiological processes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/physiological-process "Learn more about physiological processes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") from metabolism to reproduction through epigenetic processes.[23](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1550413123004473#bib23) Stress or trauma can promote epigenetic change associated with neurological change and disease susceptibility in many organisms including humans.[24](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1550413123004473#bib24) Environmental impacts on biology have shown that early-life [fetal development](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/fetus-development "Learn more about fetal development from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and early postnatal life is more sensitive to environmentally induced alterations to promote later-life pathology conditions. Pubertal periods are also sensitive for developing organs such as the breast and gonads. Adults are less sensitive but can be influenced with longer term exposures and dramatic exposures (e.g., high chemical concentrations).[6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1550413123004473#bib6) These early-life exposures directly impact the epigenetics of the developing cells to reprogram the epigenetics and allow the effects of these early-life exposures to persist throughout adult life. Therefore, your metabolic disease as an adult may be more a factor of your early-life exposure than the exposures you have as an adult. Although dietary parameters clearly impact the adult metabolic disease and obesity, the early-life environmental exposure may program the ability of an individual to respond to the adult exposures of diet, or other factors such as exercise,[25](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1550413123004473#bib25) which has also been assessed in twin studies.[26](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1550413123004473#bib26) These environmental impacts are mediated through epigenetics, which then impacts the gene expression, physiology, and disease susceptibility of the individual.

The environmental regulation of epigenetic programming of [cell differentiation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/cellular-differentiation "Learn more about cell differentiation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") early in life will reprogram the cell differentiation so that these alterations impact adult cell populations function such that the pathology impact persists or is indirectly influenced from the early-life period. The cascade of gene expression observed during cell differentiation is also directly regulated in response to epigenetic cascades during development. The analysis of epigenetics and gene expression at various stages of development reveals more differences between the stages of development than similarities, both in epigenetics and gene expression.[6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1550413123004473#bib6) Although the phenotypes such as metabolic disease or obesity may manifest later in life, early-life fetal, postnatal, and pubertal exposures were equally as important to establish disease susceptibility as the adult stage of development. Therefore, analysis of epigenetics at early-life periods can provide epigenetic biomarkers for later-life disease susceptibility.

In addition to the impacts of early-life and adult environmental exposure on epigenetic programming of disease susceptibility, we now know that ancestral exposures through [epigenetic transgenerational](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/transgenerational-epigenetics "Learn more about epigenet

enriched_points.expanded_content: Epigenetic biomarkers show promise for early diagnosis and prognosis of diseases, particularly cancers, by identifying unique methylation patterns associated with specific health conditions.
Epigenetic modifications are potential therapeutic targets, enabling the development of drugs that can reverse aberrant gene expression patterns in diseases like cancer and metabolic disorders.
Utilizing epigenetic profiles allows for more personalized treatment plans, tailoring therapies based on individual epigenetic responses to improve efficacy and reduce adverse effects.
